frameworks.name: Bayesian prior-likelihood integration
frameworks.one_line: Density priors from material identity are integrated with sensory weight signals.

frameworks.name: material-weight illusion
frameworks.one_line: Classic single-material MWI extended to bipartite two-material objects.

core_question: Does the material-weight illusion persist, disappear, or invert when objects appear to be made of two different materials?
key_assumptions: Observers hold well-learned priors about densities of common materials.
Visually inferred material identity drives expected weight distribution.
Sensory weight cues from lifting are integrated with material priors.
manipulations: Single-material vs bipartite two-material visual appearance.
Uniform vs nonuniform actual weight distribution.
Judgement target: per-side vs whole-object weight.
key_findings: Bipartite uniform-mass objects produced an inverted MWI (heavier-looking side felt heavier).
Classic MWI replicated for objects appearing uniformly one material.
Whole-object weight judgements on bipartite objects yielded no illusion.
Illusions were independent of the lifting forces used.
interpretations: Perceived weight reflects integration of incoming sensory information with density priors.
Expected weight distribution from material identity shapes per-side weight percepts.
